episodes.title: The method
episodes.intro_text: You are Juror 12 at Westhaven Crown Court. The Crown alleges the defendant obtained jewellery and other valuables from several women by false pretences, posing as a wealthy gentleman with a written list of clothing and a worthless cheque. This episode opens with the first two complainants.

episodes.title: Many accounts
episodes.intro_text: Two more complainants describe the same pattern: a titled stranger, a written list, a sealed cheque, and jewellery taken for sizing. A police inspector then explains how the women were asked to pick out the accused.

episodes.title: Handwriting and history
episodes.intro_text: A handwriting expert compares the lists and cheques with papers from the accused's lodgings. A retired officer then testifies about a conviction from nearly twenty years before, as the defence begins to challenge whether the Crown has the right man.

episodes.title: The accused speaks
episodes.intro_text: The accused addresses the jury directly. The remaining exhibits are assembled, and the judge gives directions on burden, identification, expert opinion, and what you may consider about the earlier conviction.

blocks.text: I was a widow, living near Regent's Park, that December. About six o'clock one Monday evening I was standing in Bond Street, in mourning, when a well-dressed gentleman spoke to me. He said I must be a very young widow, and asked how my husband had died. After we had talked for about a quarter of an hour I gave him my address, though I rarely received gentlemen. He said he was dining out and would write. He did not give his name.

Next morning I received a letter on Grand Hotel notepaper asking me to expect him between one and two. He arrived by cab, took off his coat, and sat by the fire. He described a house in St John's Wood and offered me a place as his housekeeper — the lady before me, he said, was being sent away without money, though she kept the jewellery he had given her. He promised five pounds a week to begin with, rising to ten if I suited him, and spoke of estates in Lincolnshire and of grapes to be sent the next day.

He told me I must leave off my mourning, and he wrote out a list of the dresses and jewellery I was to order — I never suggested a single item. He named the shops, and he handed me a cheque for fifteen guineas to pay a ten-pound deposit; he had two cheques already written in his pocket. He sealed the cheque in an envelope addressed to a bank at Belgrave Mansions. When I asked whether it might be changed nearer home he refused — a gentleman in his position, he said, could not have cheques changed casually.

He asked to see my rings. He took my wedding ring and another, for sizing, and promised they would come back about five o'clock by a one-armed commissionaire from the Grand Hotel, together with jewellery he meant to buy. The rings were worth about five or six pounds. After he had gone I found a brooch missing from my dressing table. I showed the cheque to my brother, and on his advice I never presented it, and I did not go to the police at the time.

In January I read newspaper reports of a man charged with frauds like this, and I went to the police court — I had not spoken to the police first. I saw the accused in the dock and recognised him at once, from his back. I said then that I should know him among a thousand. I am quite sure he is the same man.

source.locator: NBT p. 120, examination; S-03 Morning Advertiser cross-check

blocks.text: It is true I read two or three newspaper accounts — the Standard and the People among them — before I went to the court. I knew a man was in custody for conduct like what I complained of. I was angry about my wedding ring. I stood in the street with him ten to fifteen minutes, near a lighted shop window, and I was calm at both meetings.

He was middle-aged, with light hair tinged grey, very well dressed, with a gold watch and a heavy chain. I cannot say whether he faced me in the street. At the police court I identified him by his back. I had never identified anyone before. One ring bore the motto Love and Friendship inside; the brooch was a five-shilling piece, mounted and enamelled with a date.

I attended the police court four times, and I heard one other lady give her evidence. There was nothing peculiar about his dress except the watch and chain. I saw him write the list freely, and I heard him say the departing housekeeper had jewellery worth eight thousand pounds.

In Bond Street he wore a blue overcoat with a velvet collar; when he called, a black frock coat with fawn spats. The shop was well lighted, and I saw his face clearly during a visit of an hour, an hour and a half. I thought him English, though he might have had a slight foreign accent.

I lived in St John's Wood. One July afternoon in St James's Street I was with a lady and a small boy when the accused spoke to me. He gave the name Wilton and said he would call. I did not know him. He wrote my address in a pocket book and called the next afternoon about four o'clock; he stayed three-quarters of an hour.

When he called I told him that neither I nor anyone I knew recognised the name. He said it had been an excuse to speak to me, and that he was Lord Wilton. He described an empty house in St John's Wood and suggested I move there. When I said I was happy where I was, he offered me better dresses and jewellery, and wrote a list on paper I supplied.

He arranged to return the following Monday, and gave me a cheque for thirty-five pounds in an envelope addressed to a bank in St James's Street — I was to pay a deposit with it. He asked for a ring to measure my finger, and I gave him a small diamond ring, and a dented gold bracelet he offered to have repaired. He said a one-armed commissionaire would return them within the hour.

I also showed him a marquise ring in its case, and I left the room twice — for matches, and for a photograph. After he left I missed the marquise ring. It was worth about fifteen pounds, though at first I had valued everything he took at that sum. I went to St James's Street and could find no Union Bank there.

On the ninth of July I gave the police a description. On the sixteenth of December I was sent for. I viewed seven or eight men at the station, and I recognised the accused after asking that he remove his hat. He had been with me in my rooms three-quarters of an hour; I say he is the man.

I agree I was influenced by his removing his hat when I picked him out. He was between fifty and sixty, rather short, stout, with a dark complexion and a short brown moustache turning grey. I had never seen him before in St James's Street, though I have since learned he lived nearby.

The lady with me in the street walked on; she has not attended court. When he called he wore a frock coat, a white waistcoat lining, white spats, and a heavy gold chain. He wrote normally, but he signed the cheque holding the pen between two fingers. I was sent for soon after his arrest; I had not read a description of him beforehand.

Another witness spoke to me at the police court about what I had lost. Among the men shown to me I noticed the accused at once, but I would not swear to him until his hat came off. Another man had a grey moustache, but he was too tall, with a ruddy face. In the street he wore a low round hat and, I think, a brownish coat; he dressed differently when he called.
The property could be easily identified. I valued the loss higher once I knew the marquise ring's true worth. My name and address were not found among the accused's papers.

source.locator: NBT pp. 136, re-examination (inferred from examination close)

blocks.text: In February I lived in Upper Baker Street. On a Saturday about one o'clock I was passing through Ludgate Hill with my sister when a well-dressed man — I believe him to have been the accused — asked if I was waiting for an omnibus. After a brief conversation he asked my address, and permission to call the next day, which I gave.

He called the next day about two, announced as Mr Wilton, and said he was the Earl of Wilton. He sat with me for three-quarters of an hour, wrote a list of dresses, and spoke of a house in Abbey Road, St John's Wood, with conservatories, servants, and good wine. He proposed I should dine there on the Tuesday, to see whether I liked the place.

I was to order at Redfern's, a riding habit at Cobb's in Baker Street, and boots at Norman's in Victoria Street. He tore a cheque for forty pounds from a cheque-book, wrote and signed it with his hand shielding the paper, and put it in an envelope addressed to a bank at Belgravia Mansions. He asked for my rings, to size replacements, and took my wedding ring and a plain foreign gold ring, promising their return by a commissionaire from Streeter's.

Next morning I found no London and South-Western Bank at Belgravia Mansions. I opened the envelope, and later sent the cheque to the Balham branch, which refused payment and kept it. The commissionaire never came, and my rings were not returned. I made no complaint to the police at the time.

On the second of January the police fetched me to Westminster Police Court, where I picked the accused out from about a dozen men. I touched him with my umbrella. I thought at the time of our talks that he was not English, though he claimed an English title. I have no doubt he is the man.

source.locator: NBT pp. 124, examination; Cd. 2315 p. 77 cross-check

blocks.text: The police came to me after seeing my name on the cheque — that is so. I had been with him only about two minutes in the street. My sister saw him there, but she has not attended court. I read about the case in the papers after I was subpoenaed, and I knew a man was in custody.

The accused looks slightly thinner now, and his moustache is not waxed as it was before, but I remain pretty sure of him. He was remarkably well dressed, with spats and a black frock coat, a black satin tie with a large pearl pin, and something white inside his waistcoat. I cannot say whether he had a watch and chain.

Five or six women attended to identify him; we went in one by one, and I went first. I hesitated only because I disliked the task. I did not touch anyone else. When he wrote the dress list he wrote freely but indistinctly, holding his pen backwards.

I was the first to identify him, and I was surprised later to see Miss Taylor among the witnesses. We had not spoken about the case beforehand, and neither of us helped the other to point him out. Apart from being thinner, and the moustache, he looks much as he did in February and at the police court.

I am a widow, living in a flat in Shaftesbury Avenue. On the sixth of March, about a quarter past one, I was walking in Piccadilly with my little daughter when the accused asked if I was Lady somebody; I said I was not. He asked where I lived, and whether I knew Lord Aberdeen, and I thought from that I had met him before. He kept a handkerchief to the left side of his face for about three minutes.

He called the next afternoon about twenty to four, again partly concealing his face while my sister admitted him. He said he was Lord Winton de Willoughby, had just returned from Canada, and had stayed with the Earl of Aberdeen. He offered a house in St John's Wood with twelve servants, a carriage, and a wine cellar, and dictated a list of clothes I should order, including items from Redfern's.

He wrote a cheque for one hundred and twenty pounds, sealed it in an envelope, and promised jewellery from Streeter's. I gave him my wedding ring for sizing, and lent him a thick gold curb bracelet, my sister's bracelet, an ostrich-feather fan, a pair of elephant tusks, and a hand-painted porcelain photograph. He said a one-armed commissionaire would return the ring.

I left the room to fetch tea. Two or three hours after he left I missed tiger claws, and the teeth of an animal mounted in silver with my monogram. I valued what he took at about one hundred and eighty pounds. Next day the Balham bank kept the cheque, and it was dishonoured. Two days later I reported the matter at Vine Street and gave a description.

I did not see him again until late December, when the police brought me to Westminster Police Court. I viewed thirteen men in the yard and at once recognised the accused. When I heard him speak I was doubtful, because in my flat he had a Yankee twang, but in court he used a foreign accent I thought Swiss. His hands, hair, and feet seemed the same.

I might have met him in India, where I lived eleven years, but I did not recognise him from that. In Piccadilly he wore a black overcoat with a velvet collar, and spats. When he called it was very dark, and he kept the handkerchief to his face while my sister let him in.

I made the articles into a parcel for him. None was found in his possession, though I could identify them. No other man shown with him was like him. I read the case in the papers, but I had spoken to the police before that. He was about fifty-three, with no whiskers and the same moustache as now.

He seemed to write freely, though I did not watch closely, because his back was towards me. He appeared to have a gold watch and chain. I gave the police a full list of my property. He looks just the same now as then. He fidgeted at my flat, and refused the tea prepared for him.

At the police court he wore an overcoat like the man's, and when he called he had fawn-coloured spats. At the station I heard him speak a foreign language to an interpreter; it was not French, and might have been Norwegian. I felt he wanted to leave as quickly as possible.

I took charge of this case after the third remand, in January, succeeding a local inspector who had handled the earlier hearings. Before that I was not responsible for the accused's clothing being left at his lodgings, which I would not have allowed.

After taking charge I conducted the remaining identifications at Westminster Police Court, including Mrs Elling and three other complainants whose cases were still outstanding. I chose men to stand with him resembling the accused as far as possible, I gave the women no hint, and I let the accused place himself where he liked among fourteen or sixteen others.

Several women identified the accused under my supervision. I noticed they seemed to feel sympathy for him on account of his age. Two women had failed to identify at an earlier December hearing, and five in all failed, while others identified him but would give no names or addresses.

I do not regard identification in the dock alone as sufficient unless it is supported by other identifications. Suitable members of the public cannot be compelled to stand in a line; we had to take whom we could find in the street and the adjoining shops.

I searched the accused's box at the Covent Garden Hotel. I found white and brown spats, several white waistcoats, underwear, an opera hat, a wedding ring, photographs of ladies and views of Norway and Sweden, and a large quantity of business correspondence.

I understood the accused had stayed at the hotel until the previous September, and later at the Buckingham Hotel in the Strand. I did not go to Victoria Street, and I took charge only after the third of January. The waistcoats produced are typical of those found.

I made inquiries about a Mrs Gardiner, who had been convicted of passing bad cheques and of pretending to be a person of distinction, with a warrant out against her for assault, later withdrawn by her landlady. Despite every effort I could not find her to subpoena as a witness.
I repeat that I was not in charge until the third remand, and I would not have left the prisoner's clothes at his lodgings. I made every effort to trace Mrs Gardiner for the defence.

I am a handwriting expert of many years' standing, at Holborn Viaduct. I examined the cheques and promissory-note forms, a manuscript book found at the Covent Garden Hotel, and three letters sworn to be the prisoner's writing, to Mr Chetwynd. They are all in the same hand.

The lists and cheques are not in the prisoner's ordinary hand. Two disguises have been adopted: a back-handed or vertical scribble on the signatures, address lists, and envelopes; and a larger distorted ordinary hand, more resembling his writing in the books.

I prepared a report with facsimiles showing the similarities, and I have pointed them out in court. Comparing the specimens from the clothing lists and cheques with the writing attributed to the accused, I cannot be mistaken.

The writing is that of a foreigner — the Scandinavian type, which would include Norway — distinct from the usual English type. I have again indicated the matching letter-forms on the documents before you.

I agree the dress lists were written with freedom, and that control of the fingers was not exercised; the arm did the work. The photographs in my report are much reduced. I do not suggest the accused held his pen differently for those lists.

I was asked whether exhibits from an earlier fraud trial, eighteen years before, were in the same writing. I was not permitted to answer: prosecuting counsel objected that this raised a collateral issue, and the judge ruled the question inadmissible — whether the accused was the man convicted then was a distinct matter that might mislead the jury if introduced before verdict.

source.locator: NBT pp. 171–172, ruling on cross-examination; Cd. 2315 pp. 108–109

blocks.text: When pressed whether I could still be mistaken, allowing for disguised writing, I said the whole of the documents put to me were undoubtedly in the same handwriting. I was perfectly satisfied, and could give my reasons if they were wanted.

source.locator: Cd. 2315 pp. 116, 290; NBT p. 221, report extract

blocks.text: I live in Marylebone, and I am pensioned from the Metropolitan Police.

Eighteen years ago I was stationed at Hunter Street, and I was present at the central criminal court when a prisoner was convicted of felony under a false name — stealing earrings, a ring, and money — and sentenced to five years' penal servitude.
I produce the certificate of that conviction. The prisoner in the dock is the man; I took him into custody at Euston Road.

The prisoner then represented himself as Lord Willoughby. I could not find where he had been living. Nothing was found out about him beyond addresses in seventeen similar cases on that occasion. When charged he refused his address.
I was asked whether the same man might have committed these frauds while the accused was known abroad. I know what is at stake, and without doubt the prisoner is the man I arrested.

Counsel then sought to show that the man imprisoned from the spring of that earlier year until four years later could not be the accused now before the court. The judge intervened: that, he ruled, was not for the jury on the present indictment — the earlier conviction could not be litigated as proof of a different offender until after verdict, lest it confuse the issue.
From beginning to end of these charges I have had nothing to do with them. I am absolutely innocent.
He had lived for years at the Covent Garden Hotel and later at the Buckingham Hotel in the Strand, then in rooms in Victoria Street sub-let to him alone.

source.locator: NBT pp. 150–155, defence evidence; hotel witnesses

blocks.text: He was engaged in mining business, attended a solicitor's office in Cornhill almost daily, received substantial cheques for shares, and improved his dress as his affairs prospered — not from cheating women.

source.locator: NBT pp. 151–153, Kistner and hotel evidence

blocks.text: When arrested he told police it was a great mistake; he had never seen the ladies before in his life. He denied being the titled gentleman they described.
In the autumn he sent his wardrobe to be cleaned because he was going to Norway to survey mining property; his clothes had been shabby earlier in the year.
Those who knew him daily said he wrote very badly and slowly, holding the pen in a peculiar way; his natural writing was laboured, not the rapid lists described.

source.locator: NBT pp. 151–152, chambermaid and clerk evidence

blocks.text: Cross-examined, he was asked about the foreign accent several complainants noticed. He spoke with a Scandinavian origin and Spanish from years abroad; he had asked to speak to the jury so they might hear him directly.

source.locator: NBT p. 178, permission to speak; complainant cross themes

blocks.text: He was asked about white spats, waistcoats, and fine dress matching the descriptions. He said hotel staff never saw him in white spats or a white waistcoat; his dress improved only in the summer after mine payments.

source.locator: NBT pp. 150–153, defence hotel and tailor evidence

blocks.text: On the handwriting expert, he said disguised writing on cheques was not how he wrote letters at the hotel; a clerk who saw him daily could not swear to his hand on the disputed documents.
He maintained his innocence. His case was that the man convicted long ago and imprisoned for years was the same person who had carried out these frauds, and he had been mistaken for that man.

source.locator: NBT p. 178, defence application on prior indictments

exhibits.title: Clothing list (Mrs Elling)
exhibits.text: Handwritten list headed dresses and mourning wear to be ordered, including bonnets (spelled bonets in the original), silk gowns, gloves, and mourning trim. The accused specified named shops including a court dressmaker.
exhibits.prosecution_claim: Shows the method of building trust through an elaborate wardrobe order.
exhibits.defence_claim: The list proves nothing about identity or intent to defraud.

media.caption: Usher presents the clothing list to the jury.

source.locator: Cd. 2315 facsimile; NBT p. 119 list Q

exhibits.title: Clothing list (Mrs Garner)
exhibits.text: Second list of dresses and accessories written for Mrs Garner on paper she supplied, with items to be ordered before a further appointment on the following Monday.
exhibits.prosecution_claim: Repeats the same fraudulent pattern used against Mrs Elling.
exhibits.defence_claim: A list alone does not show the accused wrote it or meant to cheat.

source.locator: NBT pp. 135–136, list in Daisy Grant examination

exhibits.title: Handwriting comparison chart
exhibits.text: Chart comparing specimens from the clothing lists and cheques with writing attributed to the accused. The expert marked several letter-forms as identical and said he could not be mistaken.
exhibits.prosecution_claim: The same hand wrote the lists, cheques, and accused samples.
exhibits.defence_claim: Comparison charts oversimplify natural variation in handwriting.

source.locator: Cd. 2315 appendix facsimiles; expert evidence pp. 89–90

exhibits.title: Sealed cheque and envelope
exhibits.text: Representative cheque torn from a pocket book, signed with the pen held between two fingers, and placed in a sealed envelope addressed to a bank branch at a grand mansion address. Complainants were told not to present it casually.
exhibits.prosecution_claim: The cheques were worthless props to obtain jewellery and deposits.
exhibits.defence_claim: A cheque in an envelope does not prove who wrote it or that it was meant to defraud.

exhibits.title: Schedule of complainants' encounters
exhibits.text: Condensed schedule of how each complainant met the gentleman, how long between first contact and the taking of rings or cheques, and when police became involved. Intervals range from a single afternoon to several days.
exhibits.prosecution_claim: The same rapid method was repeated against multiple women.
exhibits.defence_claim: Brief meetings and varying intervals weaken identification and intent.

exhibits.title: Identification parade record
exhibits.text: Record of identifications at Westminster Police Court: the accused stood among fourteen or sixteen other men of roughly similar appearance, chose his own place, and several complainants picked him out while others had failed at earlier hearings.
exhibits.prosecution_claim: Multiple independent identifications under police supervision.
exhibits.defence_claim: Line-ups used whoever was available; sympathy and suggestion may have tainted picks.

exhibits.title: Chronology of key dates
exhibits.text: Timeline juxtaposing the accused's prior conviction eighteen years earlier, his hotel stay until the autumn before the charges, the mixed police identifications in winter, and the expert's certainty about handwriting. The jury must weigh whether old guilt and modern science outweigh weak first meetings.
exhibits.prosecution_claim: Pattern, identification, and handwriting converge on guilt.
exhibits.defence_claim: Stale prior guilt and doubtful ID procedures create reasonable doubt.

exhibits.title: Papers from the accused's lodgings
exhibits.text: Items seized from the accused's hotel box: formal clothing, a wedding ring, photographs, a quantity of business letters, and envelopes addressed to bank branches that complainants later could not find.
exhibits.prosecution_claim: Props and correspondence show a prepared fraud, not a gentleman's errand.
exhibits.defence_claim: Personal effects and post do not prove the taking charged in court.

directions.title: Burden and standard
directions.text: The Crown must prove each charge so you are sure before you convict. The accused need prove nothing. You must decide only on the evidence you have heard, not on speculation.

source.locator: NBT trial record; Westhaven adaptation AD-2

directions.title: Identification caution
directions.text: Identification evidence calls for special care. Several women picked the accused from police lines after brief or dark meetings, and some failed to identify. Consider whether excitement, sympathy for an elderly man, or suggestion may have affected them. This caution is given in modern Westhaven form; the historical trial predated today's standard warnings.

directions.title: Expert evidence
directions.text: You have heard opinion from a handwriting expert. Experts may assist you, but the decision remains yours. Consider whether disguised writing, comparison charts, and certainty of phrase are enough to link the lists and cheques to the accused beyond reasonable doubt.

source.locator: Cd. 2315 expert evidence pp. 290+; NBT Gurrin testimony

directions.title: Prior-allegation ruling
directions.text: You heard a retired officer speak of a conviction eighteen years earlier, and the defence sought to show another man was imprisoned then. The judge ruled that this earlier guilt could not be tried as part of the present indictment. You must not treat that conviction as proof of guilt here; consider only whether the Crown has proved this case on its own evidence.

source.locator: Cd. 2315 pp. 108–109; NBT ruling on prior conviction (§8.6.5)

ground_truth.note: The Crown's case depends on confident eyewitness identification, yet the complainants concede the encounters were brief and the caller partly concealed his face, and that they had read newspaper reports and attended the police court together before picking him out. Inspector Vale himself would not treat identification in the dock as sufficient without independent support. The tension goes directly to whether the jury can be sure the accused is the man.

Mrs Garner's stated value of the property she lost rises between her examination and her re-examination. It is a genuine inconsistency in her evidence, but immaterial to the charge: it touches neither the identity of the caller nor whether a false pretence was made.

source.locator: Cd. 2315 (complainant evidence); Sessions Papers schedule of loss

ground_truth.note: It appears contradictory that those who know the accused say he writes slowly and badly while the fraud documents were written with freedom. The contradiction is illusory: the handwriting expert attributes the documents to a deliberately disguised hand, so fluency in a disguised script does not conflict with the accused's ordinary laboured writing.
